coredata.dc:title: FRET-based fluorometry assay for curcumin detecting using PVP-templated Cu NCs
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Zhixia
Liu, Hanmeng
Liu, Yaosheng
Diao, Yongxing
Hu, Guangxing
Zhang, Qifang
Li, Zhuang
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorometric strategy is proposed for detecting curcumin by polyvinyl pyrrolidone-templated Cu NCs (PVP–Cu NCs) as a fluorescent probe which exhibits excitation/emission peaks at 380/510 nm. The fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of PVP-Cu NCs have a striking overlap with the UV–vis spectrum of curcumin, and the fluorescence lifetime of PVP-Cu NCs decreases after the addition of curcumin. Curcumin leads to fluorescence quenching based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer. This method allows for the determination of curcumin in the range of 0.1–10 μg mL−1 and the detection limit is 21 ng mL−1. Furthermore, this method displays good selectivity and is successfully applied for real sample analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
PVP
Fluorometry
Curcumin determination
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations and optical properties of PVP-Cu NCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescent emission spectra changes of PVP-Cu NCs with various excitation wavelengths. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescent excitation, and emission spectra of PVP-Cu NCs. The inset represents the digital photographs of PVP-Cu NCs in sunlight (left) and ultraviolet light (right).
Typical fluorescent spectra of different system solutions. The concentration of curcumin is 20 μg mL−1; pH is 6.0; the reaction time is 5 min.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcumin (a); fluorescent excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of PVP-Cu NCs. (B) Fluorescence decay spectra of PVP-Cu NCs in the absence and presence of curcumin. (C) Zeta potentials of PVP-Cu NCs and curcumin.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of the proposed system in the presence of different concentrations of curcumin. (B) The linear calibration plot for detection of curcumin. The inset displays the relationship between ΔF and the concentration of curcumin. The error bars represent the standard deviations of three replicate measurements.

Fluorescent responses at 510 nm of this assay in the presence of other interference. The concentration of curcumin was 20 μg mL−1, the concentrations of oxytetracycline, tetracycline, morin, rutin, kaempferol were 50 μM and the others were 100 μM. The error bars represent the standard deviations of three replicate measurements.
Schematic illustration of the detection principle for curcumin based on PVP-Cu NCs by FRET.
Comparison of the analytical performance of different methods for curcumin detection.

head.ce:title: FRET-based fluorometry assay for curcumin detecting using PVP-templated Cu NCs

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorometric strategy is proposed for detecting curcumin by polyvinyl pyrrolidone-templated Cu NCs (PVP–Cu NCs) as a fluorescent probe which exhibits excitation/emission peaks at 380/510 nm. The fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of PVP-Cu NCs have a striking overlap with the UV–vis spectrum of curcumin, and the fluorescence lifetime of PVP-Cu NCs decreases after the addition of curcumin. Curcumin leads to fluorescence quenching based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer. This method allows for the determination of curcumin in the range of 0.1–10 μg mL−1 and the detection limit is 21 ng mL−1. Furthermore, this method displays good selectivity and is successfully applied for real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin, a kind of diketones derived from the rhizome of turmeric, is widely used as a traditional natural pigment in food additives [1]. Additionally, it also shows many biological and pharmacological activities including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and anticancer [2]. It can be used for the treatment and prevention of diabetes, depression, arthritis, allergy, cancer, etc. [1–4]. With the increasing clinical research on its curative properties as a therapeutic agent, there is a growing need to develop analytical methods suitable for monitoring curcumin in biological samples [5–9].

To date, various kinds of analytical strategies have been reported for curcumin detection, such as high-performance liquid chromatography and mass spectrometry [5,7–10], electrochemistry [11], electrochemiluminescence [1], and fluorimetry [12,13]. Among these methods, fluorimetry has attracted extensive attention due to its high sensitivity, easy operation, wide linear range, time-saving, and the ability to real-time detecting [14–16]. Establishing a sensitive fluorimetry assay for curcumin sensing in biological fluids and foodstuff has generated increasing interests which is imperative to curcumin analysis in pharmacology, clinical therapy, and food safety supervision.

Metal nanoclusters (NCs) exhibit molecule-like properties, unique quantum size effect, and size-dependent fluorescence related to their nanometer dimension [17,18]. It owns lots of intrinsic advantages including good photo-stability, large stokes shift, and low environmental harm compared with quantum dots, organic dyes, and semiconductor nanoparticles [19–21]. There are many research efforts focused on the preparations and applications of metal NCs [18,20–23]. Among them, the applications of noble metal NCs are limited by their high cost. Cu is one of the cost-efficient and earth plentiful metal elements, Cu NCs have been developed as an attractive alternative to other metal NCs in applications of sensing, imaging, catalysis, and light devices [19,24,25].

Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) can be served as an ideal stabilizer of metal nanostructures due to its comprehensive advantages including commercial availability, low cost, good stability, and biological compatibility [19,26]. Highly luminescent PVP-templated Cu NCs (PVP–Cu NCs) with favorable stability and water solubility have been well reported [19].

Many reported fluorimetric methods for curcumin detection mainly utilized carbon dots (C-dots) and C-dots doped with heteroatoms (N, P, B, etc.) [13,27–29] as fluorescent probes. It is rarely reported that fluorescent Cu NCs are used to detect curcumin. In this study, encouraged by the inherent merits of Cu NCs and fluorescence assay, we expanded the application of PVP-Cu NCs as a fluorescent nanoprobe and proposed to use it in fluorescence detection of curcumin. PVP-Cu NCs displayed strong fluorescence at 510 nm, but the addition of curcumin resulted in the fluorescence quenching by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). And this assay can be available for curcumin detection in practical samples.

CuCl2·2H2O, curcumin, oxytetracycline, tetracycline, kanamycin sulfate, busulfan, morin, and kaempferol were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PVP was obtained from Tianjin Tiantai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Ascorbic acid, glucose, acetic acid, boric acid, phosphoric acid, and ethanol were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Rutin, Valine (Val), Phenylalanine (Phe), Tryptophan (Trp), Glycine (Gly), Tyrosine (Tyr), Glutamic acid (Glu), Serine (Ser), Lysine (Lys), and Arginine (Arg) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Penicillin and streptomycin sulfate were purchased from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). α-lactose was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were analytical grade and used directly without any purifications. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm) was made by an ultra-pure water machine and used in the whole work.

Cryotransmission electron microscope (cryo-TEM) images collected on an FEI Talos F200C high-resolution transmission electron microscope, operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were characterized by a NEXUS-470 spectrometer. KBr crystals were used for sample preparation. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by a VG ESCALAB MKII spectrometer. The XRD and XPS specimens were prepared by dripping 10 μL of the newly-made PVP-Cu NCs solution onto the surface of clean silicon pellets and dried at room temperature, respectively. Zeta potentials were measured by placing 1 mL sample aqueous solution into a folded capillary cell using Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSP through three repeated measurements, respectively. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were collected by CARY 500 UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer and RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. Steady-state fluorescence spectra were carried out by a JASCO FP-8600 phosphorescence spectrometer.

PVP-Cu NCs were synthesized according to a previous method [19] with minor modification. 1 g PVP was dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water, following by the addition of 0.5 mL CuCl2·2H2O (50 mM) and 1.5 mL ascorbic acid (0.1 M). Then the mixed solutions were heated at 90 °C and kept stirring for 21 h at a nitrogen atmosphere to avoid oxidation. Next, the resulting yellow solution of PVP-Cu NCs cooled down to ambient temperature and dialyzed for 48 h using a 14,000 molecular weight cut-off dialysis membrane, and finally stored at room temperature.

5 mg mL−1 curcumin stock solution was made by ethanol and diluted to various concentrations of curcumin. 940 μL Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solutions (pH 6.0), 50 μL PVP-Cu NCs, and 10 μL curcumin of different concentrations were mixed and reacted at room temperature for 5 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm with the slit widths of 5 nm.

10 μL of curcumin and some potentially interfering substances (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, glucose, α-lactose, Val, Phe, Trp, Gly, Tyr, Glu, Ser, Lys, Arg, oxytetracycline, tetracycline, penicillin, kanamycin sulfate, streptomycin sulfate, busulfan, morin, rutin, kaempferol) were severally added into the detecting system under the same conditions. The concentration of curcumin was 20 μg mL−1, the concentrations of oxytetracycline, tetracycline, morin, rutin, kaempferol were 50 μM and the others were 100 μM. The fluorescence intensities were measured after 5 min.

Human serum samples were from 461 clinical departments of the Chinese Peoples' Liberation Army 208 Hospital (Changchun, China). The quantitative analysis of curcumin in serum samples was carried out by the recovery method of standard additions. Typically, curcumin solutions with different known concentrations were added into 5% serum samples. 10 μL these sample solutions were mixed with 940 μL B-R buffer solutions (pH 6.0) and 50 μL of PVP-Cu NCs for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity was recorded with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

The food samples including ginger powder, curry powder, and mustard were purchased from a local supermarket (Changchun, China). The samples were pre-made into 0.1 g mL−1 solutions by ultrapure water and ethanol, respectively. These solutions were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min and then filtered to obtain homogeneous solutions for further analysis which is similar to curcumin detection in serum samples.

All of the experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines of the People's Republic of China and the ethics committee of the Chinese Peoples' Liberation Army 208 Hospital.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and optical properties of PVP-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectrum of PVP-Cu NCs is similar to the previous report [19]. As displayed in Fig.S1, the characteristic absorptions are consistent with those of PVP. The absorption peaks at around 1663 cm−1 and 1285 cm−1 are assigned to the characteristic stretching vibrations of CO and C–N, respectively. This indicates that the structure of PVP is remained, which can improve the stability of PVP-Cu NCs by the interaction of electron-donating nitrogen atoms and core copper atoms [19].

Fig.S2shows the XRD pattern of PVP-Cu NCs. It is only observed a broad peak around 21° without obvious diffraction peak of Cu NCs, which is ascribed to the low crystallinity of prepared Cu NCs and a rare population of crystallized copper nanoparticles in PVP-Cu NCs [19,30].Figure Scheme 1

Cryo-TEM characterization of PVP-Cu NCs was shown in Fig. S3. As seen in Fig. S3A, CuNCs were dispersed and loaded on PVP [19]. They are quasi-spherical with an average particle size of 5 nm, and the inter-planar spacing distance of 0.208 nm corresponds to the (111) plane of face-centered Cu nanocrystal [19].

The Cu 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum shows two peaks around 932.1 eV and 952.3 eV in Fig. S4, which are contributed to the characteristic peaks of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 of Cu0, respectively [30]. There is no peak at 942 eV, indicating the absence of Cu2+ in PVP-Cu NCs [30,31].

The fluorescent emission spectra of PVP-Cu NCs with different excitation wavelengths are recorded as seen in Fig. 1 A, and there is a maximum fluorescence intensity at 510 nm when the excitation wavelength is 380 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum, optimal fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra PVP-Cu NCs are displayed in Fig. 1B, the inset (right) shows that PVP-Cu NCs solution exhibits green fluorescence under ultraviolet light.

The principle of this fluorescent assay is illustrated in Scheme l, PVP-Cu NCs show strong fluorescence, however, the addition of curcumin results in the fluorescence quenching, the corresponding fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 2 . Thereby, the developed fluorescent system is viable for curcumin detection. The possible quenching mechanism of the sensing system was studied by spectra and fluorescence lifetime analysis. There is an obvious overlay of the UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcumin and fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of PVP-Cu NCs in Fig. 3 A. Accordingly, the fluorescence quenching might be associated with FRET or inner filter effect. In the meantime, fluorescence decay curves are also measured. As shown in Fig. 3B, the average fluorescence lifetime of PVP-Cu NCs in the absence and presence of curcumin is calculated to 5.06 ns and 0.96 ns, respectively. The change of fluorescence lifetime indicates that FRET causes the fluorescence quenching and inner filter effect is ruled out. Besides, the zeta potentials of PVP-Cu NCs and curcumin are measured as seen in Fig. 3C, the corresponding values are 0.18 and 0.67 mV, respectively. That is, the electrostatic interaction-induced static quenching is absent in this analytical system.
Several experimental factors including pH and reaction time which may affect the detection sensitivity were optimized before performance analysis.

The effect of the pH value is shown in Fig. S5. ΔF = F0–F1 is utilized as the criterion for optimization, in which F0 and F1 represent the fluorescent emission intensities at 510 nm in the absence and presence of curcumin, respectively. ΔF increases firstly and then decreases with increasing pH values from 4.0 to 10.0. ΔF reaches the largest value when the pH is 6.0. Thus, pH 6.0 of the B-R buffer solution is utilized for subsequent experiments.

The reaction time is another important factor and the corresponding results are displayed in Fig. S6. The fluorescence intensity in the presence of curcumin decreases dramatically to reach a platform and then increases slightly as prolonged time, there is the most intense fluorescence quenching when the reaction time is 5 min. Accordingly, 5 min is selected as the optimal time.

The performance of this assay for curcumin is analyzed under the optimized conditions. The changes in fluorescence intensity at 510 nm in the presence of different concentrations of curcumin were recorded. It is observed that the fluorescence intensity decreases as the increase of curcumin concentration (0–10 μg mL−1) in Fig. 4 A. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4B, ΔF increases gradually with the increased concentration of curcumin and tends to be constant after 20 μg mL−1 ΔF value is proportional to the concentration of curcumin ranged from 0.1 to 10 μg mL−1, the linear regression equation is ΔF = 70.2783 c + 9.1270 (R2 = 0.9954,
F ˆ
= 3047), as seen in Fig. 4B. Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) are necessary to assess detection capabilities and are important for some practical applications [32]. A signal-to-noise of 3 is widely used to estimate LOD [16,21,33], that is, LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of blank tests, and S represents the slope of linear calibration plot [27]. LOQ is also estimated based on a signal-to-noise of 10 [33]. LOD and LOQ for curcumin by this method are calculated to be 21 ng mL−1 and 71 ng mL−1, respectively.

Moreover, we compared this assay with other existing detection methods and the results are displayed in Table 1 , such as electrochemiluminescence [1], electrochemistry [6,34], and other fluorometric methods [13,27–29]. It is seen that the linear concentration range and LOD value of this assay are comparable or even superior to some previous works. Furthermore, shorter detection time makes this assay more rapid and attractive for real-time detecting. The reproducibility is investigated by six parallel tests with 10 μg mL−1 curcumin. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is 1.0%, implying the reliability of this assay.

Selectivity is an important indicator to assess the practicability of analytical approaches. The selectivity of the detecting system was investigated by various interfering substances as comparative studies. Among these interferences, some common saccharides and metallic ions (glucose, α-lactose, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+) and amino acids (Val, Phe, Trp, Gly, Tyr, Glu, Ser, Lys, Arg), which exist in biological fluids may affect the fluorescence quenching of curcumin. Besides, some drugs may also disturb the detection results, such as some antibiotics (oxytetracycline, tetracycline, penicillin, kanamycin sulfate, streptomycin sulfate), and several potential anticarcinogens with similar effects or structures to curcumin (busulfan, morin, rutin, kaempferol) were also studied. As shown in Fig. 5 , compared with the addition of curcumin, the effects of other substances on the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm are slight, even negligible. This indicates that the proposed system shows acceptable selectivity which provides certain potentials for practical application.

Some reports indicated that the determination of pure curcumin in human plasma samples by oral administration was restricted [5,7]. The chemical compositions of plasma are more complex than that of serum, plasma contains clotting factors and anticoagulants that are not present in serum [35], which may interfere with the analysis of target analytes. It was also reported that serum samples are highly similar in chemical components and analyte abundances, but there is a larger variance in plasma samples [36]. Thus, in this study, serum was selected for real sample analysis.

The effective dilution of serum before detection analysis is helpful to reduce the serum density and the interference of the matrix [33,37]. It was reported that 5% serum could be used as real samples to assess applicability [38], accordingly, we used 5% serum samples to investigate the practicability of our proposed assay for detecting curcumin. Before the test, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of diluted serum was measured. There is no obvious absorption peak in the range of 300–700 nm as seen in Fig. S7, suggesting that inner filter effect is negligible for curcumin in serum samples. The recovery experiments were carried out and the related analysis results are seen in Table 2 . The good recoveries and acceptable RSD values show that this assay can be efficiently employed for curcumin sensing in biological applications.

Moreover, this PVP-Cu NCs-based fluorimetric platform was also applied to curcumin sensing in food samples. As shown in Table S1, the initial concentrations of curcumin in aqueous solutions of ginger powder, curry powder, and mustard were detected directly by this method, respectively. The results are similar to the previous report [27]. Besides, consider that these food samples are more soluble in ethanol, we also detected the curcumin amounts in ethanol sample solutions which were displayed in Table 3 . Curcumin recoveries in these food samples were investigated by 5 μg mL−1 spiked curcumin and ranged from 96% to 102% within allowable RSD values. These results indicate the application prospect of this method in food inspection.

Based on the fact that curcumin can quench the fluorescence of PVP-Cu NCs by FRET, a novel fluorimetric assay for curcumin detection has been proposed. Although the synthesis of PVP-Cu NCs needs a longer time and the detection relies on the instrument, the distinctive merits of this method can be ascribed to four aspects as follows. (1) PVP-Cu NCs are prepared by a one-step method without precursor synthesis and possess good water-solubility and stability. (2) The detection process is rapid and easily operated by mixing component solutions directly in a short time. (3) The method shows high sensitivity with LOD of 21 ng mL−1 and good selectivity towards some other flavonoids. (4) This method is also suitable to monitor and analyze curcumin levels in biological and food samples with desirable recoveries. Accordingly, this approach can commendably provide a sensitive detection platform for curcumin and allow for potential applications in bioanalysis and foodstuff. Moreover, this fluorescent assay not only might be helpful for the future establishing more convenient and efficient strategies for real-time and visual curcumin detection but also may exploit new ideas for the development of bioassays to improve the bioavailability of curcumin.

Zhixia Yao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data Curation, Writing-Original Draft, Writing-Review and Editing. Hanmeng Liu: Writing-Review and Editing. Yaosheng Liu: Writing-Review and Editing. Yongxing Diao: Writing-Review and Editing. Guangxing Hu: Writing-Review and Editing. Qifang Zhang:Writing-Review and Editing. Zhuang Li: Validation, Resources, Writing-Review and Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFD1100503).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121741.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence "turn-off" detection of curcumin via energy transfer using luminol-doped silica nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhao, W. Qi, Y. Fu, H. He, D. Wu, L. Qi, R. Li, Electrochemiluminescence "turn-off" detection of curcumin via energy transfer using luminol-doped silica nanoparticles, Microchim. Acta 186 (2019) 409.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioavailability of curcumin problems and promises

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Preetha, B.K. Ajaikumar, A.N. Robert, B.A. Bharat, Bioavailability of Curcumin Problems and Promises, Mol. Pharmaceut. 4 (2007) 807-818.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M. Mohandas, D.C. Desai, Epidemiology of digestive tract cancers in India. V. Large and small bowel, Indian J. Gastroenterol. 18 (1999) 118-121.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of curcumin on normal and tumor cells: role of glutathione and bcl-2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An LC-MS/MS method for simultaneous determination of curcumin, curcumin glucuronide and curcumin sulfate in a phase II clinical trial

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.R. Kunati, S. Yang, B.M. William, Y. Xu, An LC-MS/MS method for simultaneous determination of curcumin, curcumin glucuronide and curcumin sulfate in a phase II clinical trial, J. Pharmaceut. Biomed. Anal. 156 (2018) 189-198.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel sensitive electrochemical sensor based on nickel chloride solution modified glassy carbon electrode for curcumin determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Zokhtareh, M. Rahimnejad, A novel sensitive electrochemical sensor based on nickel chloride solution modified glassy carbon electrode for curcumin determination, Electroanalysis 30 (2018) 921-927.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pharmacokinetics of curcumin conjugate metabolites in healthy human subjects

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validated HPLC–MS/MS method for simultaneous determination of curcumin and piperine in human plasma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.M. Wang, Q.Z. Zhang, J. Yang, R.H. Zhu, J. Zhang, L.J. Cai, W.X. Peng, Validated HPLC-MS/MS Method for Simultaneous Determination of Curcumin and Piperine in Human Plasma, Trop. J. Pharm. Res. 11 (2012) 621-629.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A high-throughput quantification method of curcuminoids and curcumin metabolites in human plasma via high-performance liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Cao, R.X. Xu, Z. Liu, A high-throughput quantification method of curcuminoids and curcumin metabolites in human plasma via high-performance liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry, J. Chromatogr. B 949-950 (2014) 70-78.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of curcumin and resveratrol in lipidic nanoemulsion formulation and rat plasma using HPLC: optimization and application to real samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nasr, M.H. Abdel Rahman, Simultaneous determination of curcumin and resveratrol in lipidic nanoemulsion formulation and rat plasma using HPLC: optimization and application to real samples, J. AOAC Int. 102 (2019) 1095-1101.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reduced graphene oxide nanosheets modified with nickel disulfide and curcumin nanoparticles for non-enzymatic electrochemical sensing of methyl parathion and 4-nitrophenol

Wang, Polyhydric polymer-loaded pyrene composites as powerful adsorbents and fluorescent probes: highly efficient adsorption and test strips-based fluorimetric analysis of curcumin in urine and plant extracts, Analyst 143 (2018) 392-395.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots, and their applications in curcumin sensing, fluorescent inks, and super-resolution nanoscopy

Commun. 53 (2017) 9466-9469.

Chem. 86 (2014) 11714-11721.
